risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families were stratified at each tribal community on the basis of child BMI percentile for age and gender... Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used to ensure that there was an equal number of families in the intervention and control groups." Comment: The study describes stratified, blocked randomization with families randomly assigned within strata, which is sufficient detail to support a properly random allocation sequence.

Quote: "Randomization at the family level was done after obtaining consent from and completing baseline measurements on participating families. Families were stratified at each tribal community... Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used..." Comment: The article describes when and how families were randomized (stratified/blocked randomization), but it does not report any allocation concealment mechanism such as central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes. Therefore, it is unclear whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "During Year 1, mentors visited intervention families to deliver a family-based tool kit in 12 home visits... During Year 1, control families received educational tool kits and incentives sent home on the same cycle as intervention families, but they did not receive any mentoring." Comment: The intervention and control conditions were visibly different, so participants and personnel delivering the intervention (mentors) could not realistically be blinded to allocation. This knowledge could plausibly influence several study outcomes, especially self-reported and behavior-related outcomes such as dietary intake, TV/screen time, health behavior efficacy, and health-related quality of life. Therefore performance bias is at high risk overall, despite some objective outcomes also being measured.

Quote: "During Year 1, mentors visited intervention families to deliver a family-based tool kit in 12 home visits... During Year 1, control families received educational tool kits and incentives sent home on the same cycle as intervention families, but they did not receive any mentoring." Quote: "Three questionnaires were completed by adult primary caregivers... The SF-12v2 Health Survey... is a widely used 12-item instrument for assessing self-perceived health-related quality of life..." Quote: "Screen time was assessed... by the inclusion of 4 questions asking how much time the TV was on in the home..." Comment: Outcomes were assessed in part by patient/primary-caregiver self-report (the participant is the outcome assessor for questionnaires and reported behaviors such as screen time; dietary recalls for children were also obtained via caregiver/teacher proxies). Because the intervention involved obvious home mentoring versus mailed materials, participants were not plausibly blinded to allocation. At least one reported outcome is subjective/self-reported (e.g. quality of life, health behavior efficacy, screen time, dietary recall), so knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. By the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective self-reported outcomes makes this domain High risk overall, even though some other outcomes were objective (e.g. anthropometrics, accelerometry, lab measures).

Quote: "A recruitment goal of 150 adult–child pairs was set in order to retain 120 families." and "Limitations include the difficulty in retaining families for 2 years..." Comment: This article describes the trial design and anticipated retention challenges, but it does not report actual post-randomization numbers analysed at 1 or 2 years by arm, nor reasons for missing outcome data on the primary outcomes (child BMI z-score, adult BMI). Planned/expected retention is not observed attrition, and without per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers at the analysed time points, attrition bias cannot be judged as low or high.
